metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the schema constant naming in the plan: the existing constant is `SCHEMA` (not `SCHEMA_FAIL`); either rename it to `SCHEMA_FAIL` and update all three references (`SCHEMA`, `latest_fail_verdict`'s guard, and the test assertion `rec['schema'] == 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'`), or keep `SCHEMA` as-is and add `SCHEMA_PASS` alongside it — but the plan as written claims a non-existent name.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged (so `latest_fail_verdict` and the existing FAIL-record tests are untouched)'
completion_sidecar.py line 29: `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` — the constant is named SCHEMA, not SCHEMA_FAIL
Search for 'SCHEMA_FAIL' across src/rebar/llm returns zero matches
`latest_fail_verdict` references `SCHEMA` (line 133: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`), not a `SCHEMA_FAIL` name
findings.finding: The plan refers to 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged' and 'add `SCHEMA_PASS`', but the actual constant in `completion_sidecar.py` is named `SCHEMA` (line 29: `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"`), NOT `SCHEMA_FAIL`. No `SCHEMA_FAIL` constant exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` bullet; Schema-version strategy section
scenarios: An implementer following the plan literally would add `SCHEMA_PASS` alongside a new `SCHEMA_FAIL` constant, leaving the existing `SCHEMA` constant unreferenced and breaking `latest_fail_verdict`'s schema guard.
Alternatively, if they rename `SCHEMA` to `SCHEMA_FAIL`, every existing reference (line 29, 133, tests at test_completion_gate.py:538) breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: In `completion_sidecar.py`: keep the existing `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` constant name unchanged (rename it to `SCHEMA_FAIL` only if all downstream references are updated simultaneously); add `SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"` as a second constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` to expect `{'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'}` in `cv.model_fields` (and add a schema-property assertion for `criteria`) after the `CompletionVerdict` field addition.
evidence: Plan: 'add a `Criterion` sub-model … and a `criteria: list[Criterion] = Field(default_factory=list)` field to `CompletionVerdict`'
Plan: 'so the model↔schema PARITY test (`tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py::test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema`) still passes'
test line 55: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` — this strict set-equality will fail once 'criteria' is added to the model
findings.finding: The plan claims `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` 'still passes' after adding `criteria` to `CompletionVerdict`, but the test asserts `set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` — once a `criteria` field is declared on the model, this exact set-equality check will fail, breaking the test the plan explicitly names as preserved.
scenarios: `make test` run after implementing the contracts.py change — the parity test fails immediately because 'criteria' is in model_fields but not in the expected set {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 55 in the test from `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` to `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'}`, and add an assertion that `'criteria' in schema['properties']` to keep the model↔schema parity check honest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit edit target for `tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py` — update the hard equality assertions (`set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}`) in `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` and `test_response_model_for_selects_by_output_schema` to include `'criteria'` after adding the new field to `CompletionVerdict`.
evidence: Plan quote: 'add the OPTIONAL `criteria[]` property … so the model↔schema PARITY test (`tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py::test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema`) still passes'
test_completion_verifier.py line 55: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` — this is a HARD equality, not a subset check
test_completion_verifier.py line 41-44: also asserts `set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` via the response_model_for test
Adding `criteria: list[Criterion] = Field(...)` to CompletionVerdict adds 'criteria' to model_fields, making both assertions fail
findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding `criteria[]` to `CompletionVerdict` leaves `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` 'still passing', but that test contains a hard equality assertion `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` (line 55) that would fail the moment `criteria` is added as a model field.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json` bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: An implementer adds `criteria` to `CompletionVerdict` per the plan's scope edit, runs `make test`, and `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` fails with `AssertionError: {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'} != {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}`.
`test_response_model_for_selects_by_output_schema` also fails for the same reason.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` to `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'}` in both affected tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` (line 55) to include `'criteria'` in the expected model_fields set, or relax to a `>=` check, to account for the new `CompletionVerdict.criteria` field.
evidence: test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema line 55: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}`
Plan: 'so the model↔schema PARITY test (tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py::test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema) still passes'
Adding `criteria: list[Criterion]` to `CompletionVerdict` would change model_fields to {'verdict','findings','summary','criteria'}, failing the equality assertion
findings.finding: The existing schema-pin test `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` in tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py (line 55) asserts `set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` — an EQUALITY check. Adding `criteria` to `CompletionVerdict` will break this assertion. The plan states the test 'still passes' but provides no instruction to update this assertion.
scenarios: `make test` fails immediately after the contracts.py edit because the parity test's equality assertion breaks
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 55 to: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'}` (or use `<=` if future fields are anticipated).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the internal `SCHEMA` reference on line 133 of `completion_sidecar.py` as an edit target that must be updated to `SCHEMA_FAIL` when the constant is renamed.
evidence: completion_sidecar.py line 29: `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'`
completion_sidecar.py line 133: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`

Plan says: 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged' and 'Add a SECOND constant `SCHEMA_PASS`' — implying a rename from SCHEMA to SCHEMA_FAIL, but line 133's internal reference is not mentioned
findings.finding: The plan renames the existing `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` constant to `SCHEMA_FAIL`, but `latest_fail_verdict` in the same file references `SCHEMA` by its module-level name on line 133 (`payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`). This is an unenumerated internal update — if the constant is renamed without updating that reference, `latest_fail_verdict` will reference an undefined name.
scenarios: `latest_fail_verdict` raises `NameError: name 'SCHEMA' is not defined` at runtime after the rename if line 133 is not updated
findings.suggested_fix: In the scope section for completion_sidecar.py, add: 'Update the internal `SCHEMA` reference in `latest_fail_verdict` (line 133) to `SCHEMA_FAIL`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Also update the model_fields equality assertion in `test_response_model_for_selects_by_output_schema` (line 42) to include `'criteria'` when enumerating test updates required by the contracts.py change.
evidence: test line 41-42: `cv = contracts.response_model_for('completion_verdict')` then `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}`
This is a SEPARATE test from test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema; both will fail on the same model-field change
findings.finding: The test `test_response_model_for_selects_by_output_schema` in tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py (line 42) also asserts `set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` for the `completion_verdict` contract — a second equality assertion that will break when `criteria` is added. This is a second unenumerated test consumer of the `CompletionVerdict` model fields.
scenarios: `make test` fails on two tests, not just one, after the contracts.py edit
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 42 to: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the `completion_sidecar.py` scope entry that `SCHEMA` must be RENAMED to `SCHEMA_FAIL` (not merely 'kept unchanged') and enumerate all internal references that must be updated (`latest_fail_verdict` line 133, `build_payload` line 29, and `SCHEMA =` declaration line 29).
evidence: Plan: 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged (so `latest_fail_verdict` and the existing FAIL-record tests are untouched). Add a SECOND constant `SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"`'
Codebase: completion_sidecar.py line 29: `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` — the constant is named `SCHEMA`, not `SCHEMA_FAIL`
Codebase: completion_sidecar.py line 133: `if isinstance(payload, dict) and payload.get("schema") == SCHEMA:` — `latest_fail_verdict` references `SCHEMA` by name
Codebase: completion_sidecar.py line 29 and build_payload body reference `SCHEMA` directly
findings.finding: The plan refers to 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged' and 'add `SCHEMA_PASS`', but the actual constant in `completion_sidecar.py` is named `SCHEMA` (line 29), not `SCHEMA_FAIL`. The plan treats this as an already-existing `SCHEMA_FAIL` name, obscuring that implementing the strategy actually requires RENAMING `SCHEMA` → `SCHEMA_FAIL` (a code change, not a no-op preservation) AND updating every internal reference that currently reads the `SCHEMA` constant — `latest_fail_verdict` (line 133) and `build_payload` (line 29) both reference it by name. If the rename is skipped, `SCHEMA_PASS` cannot be added symmetrically, and `build_payload`'s branch on verdict will use an undefined name.
scenarios: Developer follows the plan literally, adds `SCHEMA_FAIL` as a new constant alongside the existing `SCHEMA`, and `build_payload` branches on `SCHEMA_FAIL` — but `latest_fail_verdict` still compares against the old `SCHEMA` constant, breaking schema-guarded reads.
Rename is attempted without updating `latest_fail_verdict`'s reference, causing a `NameError` at runtime on the FAIL read path.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plan phrase 'Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` unchanged' with 'Rename the existing `SCHEMA` constant to `SCHEMA_FAIL` and update its two consumers (`latest_fail_verdict` and `build_payload`) to reference `SCHEMA_FAIL`; then add `SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"` alongside it.'

findings.finding: `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` in `tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py` (line 55) asserts exact field equality: `assert set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}`. Adding `criteria: list[Criterion]` to `CompletionVerdict` will break this assertion. The plan says 'the model↔schema parity test passes' but does not identify this specific test or its exact-equality assertion as needing an update.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 55 to: `assert {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary', 'criteria'} <= set(cv.model_fields)` (or use `==` with the full expected set including `'criteria'`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the leaf's dual-schema strategy (completion_verifier_fail_v1 + completion_verifier_pass_v1) with the parent epic's declared 'completion _v2' schema bump, or update the parent's Compatibility section to explicitly sanction the verdict-split naming before proceeding.
evidence: Parent says: 'Sidecar schemas are bumped (plan_review_result_v2 / code_review_result_v2 / a completion _v2); readers accept BOTH the old v1 (rendered with the new fields absent) and the new v2 records'
Leaf says: 'Do NOT bump the existing FAIL schema. Keep SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1" unchanged ... Add a SECOND constant SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"'

The parent's pattern is a single schema bump (v1→v2) per review type; the leaf substitutes a verdict-split pattern (fail_v1 / pass_v1) that is architecturally different and leaves FAIL permanently on v1 with no v2 counterpart.
findings.finding: The leaf's dual-schema strategy (SCHEMA_FAIL pinned forever to completion_verifier_fail_v1, new SCHEMA_PASS = completion_verifier_pass_v1) contradicts the parent epic's stated Compatibility section, which describes a single schema bump to a completion '_v2' that readers accept alongside the old v1. The leaf's approach leaves the FAIL path permanently on v1 with no v2 counterpart, diverging from the parent's 'v1 + v2 coexistence' reader contract.
findings.location: Leaf: 'Schema-version strategy (explicit)' section; Parent epic ba33-2310-f6c6-4bd6 Compatibility section
scenarios: A future audit reader built to the parent's 'accept v1 and v2' contract would need special-casing for the split schema approach; a reader expecting a completion_v2 for both PASS and FAIL records would not find it.
If a sibling story or the parent's own integration test asserts the completion sidecar is versioned as _v2, the leaf's _pass_v1 naming would fail that assertion.

The parent's rollback narrative ('reverting a story restores the prior payload shape') works correctly for the leaf's PASS path but does not address the permanently-unchanged FAIL path, leaving the parent's v1/v2 coexistence framing incoherent.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) adopt the parent's pattern: introduce a single `completion_verifier_v2` schema carrying both `criteria[]` and `findings`, used for all new records (PASS and FAIL), so the FAIL path also gets its v2 bump and the v1/v2 reader contract holds uniformly — OR (b) if the split-schema design is intentional and superior, update the parent epic's Compatibility section to replace 'a completion _v2' with an explicit description of the two-schema strategy, so the parent's reader contract is authoritative before the leaf re-reviews.

findings.finding: The leaf's schema-version strategy contradicts the parent's declared versioning approach. The parent (ba33) Compatibility section states 'Sidecar schemas are bumped (plan_review_result_v2 / code_review_result_v2 / a completion _v2); readers accept BOTH the old v1 … and the new v2 records' — implying a unified completion _v2 schema whose readers are backward-compatible with _v1. The leaf (e7e0) explicitly inverts this: 'Do NOT bump the existing FAIL schema. Keep SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1" unchanged … Add a SECOND constant SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"' — introducing a new type name instead of a v2 bump. The parent wins on conflict per G7, so the leaf must either align to a _v2 bump strategy or the parent must be updated to reflect the split-schema design.
findings.location: Leaf: 'Schema-version strategy (explicit)' section; Parent ba33: 'Compatibility & rollback' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimum fixture specification to AC3: name (or create) the fixture ticket, state it must have at least N≥2 evaluatable acceptance criteria, and require the E2E assertion to check `len(criteria) >= N` (not merely that the field exists).
evidence: "running the real completion verifier on a fixture ticket whose criteria are met" — no fixture named, no path given, no minimum criterion count stated
ABSENCE: no reference to an existing fixture directory or a description of the fixture's acceptance criteria that must be 'met'

AC3 proving command depends on the fixture producing a non-empty `criteria[]` — an empty fixture yields an empty array and the assertion passes vacuously (verify-command defect #4: fixture invalidity)
findings.finding: AC3 references 'a fixture ticket whose criteria are met' but does not name or describe the fixture. An executor writing the live/E2E test must either find an existing fixture or create one from scratch — and the plan gives no guidance on what shape the fixture ticket should take, which criteria it should cover, or where it lives in the test tree. This leaves a real implementation decision (fixture design) to the executor, who may make choices that produce a vacuous test (e.g., a ticket with zero evaluated criteria yields an empty `criteria[]` that technically 'passes').
scenarios: Executor creates a trivial fixture with no acceptance criteria; agent returns `criteria: []`; the assertion 'populated criteria[]' is vacuously true if not guarded by a cardinality check
Executor creates an incompatible fixture; real agent returns FAIL; E2E test always skips or fails, providing no signal
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Use fixture `tests/fixtures/tickets/sample_met_ticket.yaml` (or equivalent), which must contain at least 2 acceptance criteria. The E2E assertion must include `assert len(result["criteria"]) >= 2`.

findings.finding: AC3 (live E2E test) is explicitly skippable whenever an API key is absent, with no enforcement mechanism, minimum frequency requirement, or named child ticket to track it — meaning the new production code path (real LLM emitting `criteria[]` into the `completion_verifier_pass_v1` sidecar) can be closed as done with the E2E criterion routinely skipped and the live path never proven end-to-end.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'skipped with a recorded reason otherwise' with: 'skipped in CI without an API key, but MUST be executed and attested manually (with the run log URL recorded on this ticket) before the ticket is closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the prompt update wording that `criteria[]` must include ALL evaluated criteria regardless of `met` status (including `met=false` for failing ones), and that failing criteria appear in BOTH `criteria[]` (with `met=false`) AND `findings[]` — co-locating this dual-output rule with the output section of the prompt.
evidence: Plan: 'update the prompt: instruct the agent to emit `criteria[]` with one met-record per evaluated criterion (each with citation + kind). `findings` stays failures-only.'
Existing prompt (lines 162-181): output section covers `verdict`, `findings` (failures only), `summary` — no mention of `criteria[]` or `met` field
ABSENCE: no plan text specifies what `met=false` entries in `criteria[]` look like when `findings[]` also covers the same criterion — the dual-output protocol is unspecified
findings.finding: The prompt update instruction for `completion_verifier.md` says only "instruct the agent to emit `criteria[]` with one met-record per evaluated criterion (each with citation + kind)" but does not specify the dual-output rule: how the agent should handle criteria that are NOT MET — i.e., whether a failing criterion appears in BOTH `criteria[]` (with `met=false`) AND `findings[]` (as a failure finding). The current prompt's output section specifies `findings` as failures-only and `criteria[]` is not yet mentioned. Without an explicit instruction about the `met=false` + findings overlap, the agent may emit `criteria[]` only for passing criteria, or omit `met=false` entries entirely, producing an incomplete per-criterion record on FAIL.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` — prompt update instruction; Verdict and findings output section (lines 143-181 of the existing prompt)
scenarios: Agent on a FAIL ticket emits `criteria[]` only for the passing criteria and omits `met=false` entries — the sidecar's per-criterion array is incomplete
Agent includes `met=false` in `criteria[]` but omits it from `findings[]` — the `reconcile_verdict` flip-to-FAIL logic (`PASS with findings → FAIL`) may misfire

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the transition_close.py scope bullet how `material` is obtained for the PASS-path sidecar emit — e.g. call `current_material_fingerprint(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` best-effort (swallow errors, default to None) and pass the result.
evidence: transition_close.py _verdict_manifest() (lines 276-316) calls `current_material_fingerprint` internally and appends it to the manifest
Plan: 'add a best-effort `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=…, repo_root=repo_root)`' — the '…' leaves the source of `material` unspecified
The FAIL path uses `material=None`; the PASS path would need `current_material_fingerprint(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` called separately
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `transition_close.py` should emit the PASS sidecar with `material=…`, but the `material` fingerprint is currently computed INSIDE `_verdict_manifest` (via `current_material_fingerprint`) and is not available as a standalone value at the call site before `_verdict_manifest` runs. The plan does not specify where in `transition_close.py` to obtain the `material` value for the sidecar `emit()` call.
scenarios: Implementer emits with material=None (mirroring the FAIL path) and the PASS record lacks the material fingerprint, or calls current_material_fingerprint and errors if it fails — the plan does not specify best-effort handling

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan whether `kind` should be typed as `Literal["codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"]` in the pydantic model (and a matching `enum` in the JSON schema), or document that `str` is intentional and enforcement is prompt-only.
evidence: "The `kind` field vocabulary is the CLOSED set `{\"codebase-verifiable\", \"operator-attested\"}` (matching ADR 0043's two criterion kinds)"
"Add a `Criterion` sub-model (`{criterion: str, met: bool, citation: Citation | None, kind: str}`)" — `kind: str` is untyped despite the closed-set claim
ABSENCE: no statement of whether `Literal` or an `Enum` is required, or whether a JSON Schema `enum` constraint should be added to the schema file
findings.finding: The `Criterion` sub-model's `kind` field is typed as `str` in the plan, but the plan also calls it a 'CLOSED set' (`{"codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"}`). The plan does not state whether pydantic enforces the closed set via `Literal["codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"]` or merely documents it as convention with `str`. An executor who chooses `str` leaves the closed-set invariant unenforced; one who chooses `Literal` may need to update the JSON schema pattern accordingly. This is a defaultable gap but the plan should state the chosen approach.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` bullet; Schema-version strategy section (`kind` field vocabulary)
scenarios: Executor uses plain `str`; a future agent emits `kind: "attested"` (misspelling); pydantic accepts it silently; the closed-set invariant is violated undetected
Executor uses `Literal` without updating the JSON schema enum — the parity test fails
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Criterion sub-model spec to `kind: Literal["codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"]` and add a corresponding `"enum": ["codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"]` to the `kind` property in `completion_verdict.schema.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2 to explicitly state that the integration test exercises the full workflow YAML engine (not only a direct gate_op call), so the `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` expression is evaluated and the criteria[] array arrives in completion_reconcile's ctx.inputs — or add a separate YAML-threading assertion to AC2.
evidence: AC2 says 'closes a ticket through the gate with a stub verifier' — 'through the gate' is ambiguous: it could mean via the YAML workflow engine (exercises threading) or via a direct gate_op call (bypasses YAML threading)
The plan adds 'raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}' to the YAML — if the integration test never runs the YAML engine this threading is untested
Plan scope: 'src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml — thread raw_criteria' is a new default path for every ticket close
findings.finding: AC2's integration test uses a 'stub verifier returning the new PASS shape' — if this stub bypasses the workflow YAML engine and calls gate_ops.completion_reconcile directly with pre-wired inputs, the newly-defaulted `raw_criteria` threading in completion-verification.yaml is never exercised, letting the suite go green while the YAML threading is broken end-to-end.
scenarios: Integration test stubs the LLM but does not execute the workflow YAML engine, so the `raw_criteria` expression is never evaluated; suite is green but production closes fail to carry criteria[] through reconcile
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, add: 'The integration test must run the workflow engine against the real completion-verification.yaml (with a stubbed LLM response), assert that ctx.inputs["raw_criteria"] is non-empty inside completion_reconcile, and confirm criteria[] propagates to the emitted sidecar — NOT a direct gate_op call with pre-injected inputs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify (in the plan) the choice to multiplex PASS and FAIL records into the same `COMPLETION_VERDICT` event type rather than a separate `COMPLETION_PASS_VERDICT` event type, and address the shared-retention consequence for `prune()` — either by discriminating PASS/FAIL in the prune cap, or by accepting the trade-off with a documented rationale.
evidence: Plan: 'Do NOT bump the existing FAIL schema. Keep SCHEMA_FAIL = "completion_verifier_fail_v1" unchanged… Add a SECOND constant SCHEMA_PASS = "completion_verifier_pass_v1" for PASS records; build_payload picks the schema by verdict.'

Established pattern: REVIEW_RESULT, COMPLETION_VERDICT, TICKET_DIGEST, ENQUEUE_ENRICH each have their own event type registered in EVENT_TYPES and _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (src/rebar/_store/event_append.py, src/rebar/reducer/_version.py).
completion_sidecar.py prune(): prunes by `-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json` filename suffix, RETAIN_PER_TICKET=10, with no schema discrimination — PASS and FAIL records share the cap.
ABSENCE: plan provides no justification for same-event-type multiplexing vs. a separate event type, and does not address the shared-retention consequence.
findings.finding: The plan multiplexes both PASS (`completion_verifier_pass_v1`) and FAIL (`completion_verifier_fail_v1`) records into the single existing `COMPLETION_VERDICT` event type, differentiated only by the `schema` field. This departs from the established codebase pattern (separate event types per distinct record kind: REVIEW_RESULT, COMPLETION_VERDICT, TICKET_DIGEST), and the plan neither justifies this departure nor acknowledges its concrete consequence: the existing `prune()` function retains the last `RETAIN_PER_TICKET=10` `COMPLETION_VERDICT` events regardless of schema, meaning PASS and FAIL records compete for the same retention budget. On a ticket with repeated close attempts, FAIL sidecars could displace PASS records or vice versa. An alternative — a separate `COMPLETION_PASS_VERDICT` event type registered in EVENT_TYPES, _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, and a dedicated `latest_pass_record()` reader — follows the established prior-art pattern and keeps per-kind retention independent at the cost of three-site registration.
scenarios: A ticket with 6 failed close attempts followed by 4 successful re-close attempts accumulates 10 COMPLETION_VERDICT events; the oldest FAIL records are pruned when PASS records arrive, potentially removing audit history the FAIL sidecar is supposed to preserve.

A reader calling latest_fail_verdict() on a ticket with mostly PASS records must walk backwards past all the PASS records to find the most recent FAIL — the schema-guarded loop still works, but the O(n) walk is over a mixed corpus that the established pattern would have kept separate.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a separate `COMPLETION_PASS_VERDICT` event type following the established pattern (three-site registration: EVENT_TYPES, _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, a dedicated prune target), or (b) keep the same event type but give PASS and FAIL separate per-schema prune budgets by filtering on `schema` inside `prune()`. Document the chosen rationale in the plan's Compatibility & rollback section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `field_validator` on `Criterion.kind` in `contracts.py` that normalizes to `"codebase-verifiable"` or `"operator-attested"` (falling back to `"codebase-verifiable"` for unknown values), mirroring the `_norm_verdict` and `_norm_relation` patterns already in that file.
evidence: Plan: 'The `kind` field vocabulary is the CLOSED set {"codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"}'
Plan's Criterion shape: `{criterion: str, met: bool, citation: Citation | None, kind: str}` — no validator specified
Existing pattern in contracts.py: `_norm_verdict` validator on `CompletionVerdict.verdict`; `_norm_relation` validator on `OverlapVerdict.relation` (lines 87-94 and 140-145)
ABSENCE: no `field_validator` for `kind` is mentioned anywhere in the plan's Criterion sub-model spec
findings.finding: The `Criterion.kind` field is declared as `kind: str` with no normalizing validator, yet the plan defines it as a CLOSED enum `{"codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"}`. Unlike every other enum-constrained field in `contracts.py` (`verdict` has `_norm_verdict`, `OverlapVerdict.relation` has `_norm_relation`), no `field_validator` is specified for `Criterion.kind`. An LLM emitting `kind="codebase_verifiable"` (underscore), `kind="attested"`, or any other near-miss silently passes Pydantic validation and lands in the sidecar record unchecked.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` — `Criterion` sub-model definition (`{criterion: str, met: bool, citation: Citation | None, kind: str}`)
scenarios: LLM emits `kind: 'codebase_verifiable'` (underscore variant) — silently accepted, stored in sidecar with wrong value
`latest_pass_record` consumers filtering by kind get mismatched results if the LLM uses a near-miss spelling
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `Criterion` model: `@field_validator('kind') @classmethod def _norm_kind(cls, v: str) -> str: return v if v in {'codebase-verifiable', 'operator-attested'} else 'codebase-verifiable'`

findings.finding: The `kind` field on the `Criterion` sub-model is described only by example ('codebase-verifiable / operator-attested') with no enumerated contract or constraint; an executor cannot determine whether to implement it as a `Literal['codebase-verifiable','operator-attested']` enum, an open string, or something else.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `Criterion` definition: `kind: Literal['codebase-verifiable', 'operator-attested']` and update the prompt to constrain the agent to these two values, with 'operator-attested' reserved for criteria whose evidence lives outside the repo.

findings.finding: The `Criterion.kind` field has an undefined value vocabulary: the plan introduces `{criterion, met, citation, kind}` but never specifies the closed enum for `kind` (e.g. `"codebase-verifiable"` vs `"operator-attested"`), whether a normalizing validator is required (as exists for `verdict` via `_norm_verdict` and for `relation` via `_norm_relation` in `overlap_verdict`), or how a model near-miss (e.g. `operator_attested` without the hyphen, matching the existing prompt's warning about `[operator_attested]`) is handled.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `Criterion` sub-model, add a `@field_validator("kind")` that normalises: `return v if v in {"codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"} else "codebase-verifiable"`. In the prompt update, give the agent the exact two string literals it must use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `material=…` placeholder with a concrete, stated value (e.g., `material=material_fingerprint(ticket_id, repo_root)` referencing an existing helper, or document exactly what dict/hash to pass) so the executor can implement and test the emit call without stopping to ask.
evidence: "add a best-effort `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=…, repo_root=repo_root)`" — the `material=…` is an unresolved parameter, not a stated default
AC2: 'asserts on the emitted event JSON (via the real sidecar emit path)' — correctness of the emitted JSON depends on `material` being correctly populated
ABSENCE: no definition of what `material` represents at the transition_close call site, nor a cross-reference to an existing helper that computes it
findings.finding: The `transition_close.py` sidecar emit call is written as `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=…, repo_root=repo_root)` — the `material=…` ellipsis is a literal placeholder that defers a real design decision (what object/value to pass for `material`) to the executor. An executing agent cannot implement this line without stopping to determine what `material` should be (e.g., a hash of the ticket content, the raw ticket dict, a file path). This blocks both implementation and the AC2/AC3 proving commands, because a mis-passed `material` could cause the emit to silently fail or produce a wrong fingerprint, making the 'asserts on the emitted event JSON' assertion vacuous.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` bullet; AC2 and AC3
scenarios: Executor passes None or an empty string for material — sidecar emits an event with a bad fingerprint; integration test passes vacuously
Executor passes a wrong type — sidecar raises at runtime; best-effort swallow hides the bug; AC2 assertion on 'emitted event JSON' never fires because emit silently aborted
findings.suggested_fix: Identify the existing helper that computes the material fingerprint (likely used in the FAIL-path emit already) and name it explicitly: e.g., `material = _compute_material(resolved_id, repo_root)` — cross-reference the same call already made in the FAIL path of `transition_close.py` so the PASS path is symmetric.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight AC (or extend AC#1) that verifies the prompt file contains the required `criteria[]` instruction, e.g., a static content check test or a snapshot assertion on the rendered prompt, so prompt correctness is enforced in CI without LLM availability.
evidence: Scope says: 'update the prompt: instruct the agent to emit `criteria[]` with one met-record per evaluated criterion (each with citation + kind)'
AC#3 says: 'gated on LLM availability (skipped with a recorded reason otherwise)' — meaning CI can pass without ever exercising the prompt change
AC#1 and AC#2 use stub verifiers (no prompt validation); no AC uses a deterministic check that the prompt file contains the required instruction
findings.finding: The `completion_verifier.md` prompt update is a named scope deliverable but has no acceptance criterion with a reliable automated oracle — AC#3 (live E2E) implicitly depends on the prompt being correct but is explicitly skippable when LLM availability is absent, leaving prompt correctness unverifiable in CI.
scenarios: The prompt file is accidentally left unmodified (or reverted in a merge conflict); all unit/integration tests pass because they stub the verifier; the live E2E test is skipped in CI; the prompt omission is only discovered when real LLM runs produce empty `criteria[]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC#1: 'Also assert that `completion_verifier.md` contains the string `criteria` and references both `citation` and `kind` fields (a simple substring/regex check in a unit test is sufficient).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or update ADR 0043) documenting: (a) the two-schema sidecar strategy and its backwards-compatibility rationale, (b) the `latest_pass_record()` API contract, and (c) the `criteria[]` per-criterion record shape — and list this ADR file as an explicit edit target in the Scope section.
evidence: Plan states 'Do NOT bump the existing FAIL schema … Add a SECOND constant SCHEMA_PASS' with reasoning given inline but no ADR or doc artifact planned
Plan references 'ADR 0043's two criterion kinds' for vocabulary but no update to ADR 0043 is listed in scope
latest_pass_record() is a new public sidecar API; no doc/ADR entry in scope mentions documenting it
Scope section lists 8 edit targets — none is an ADR file or CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The plan introduces two durable architectural decisions — (1) a two-schema sidecar strategy (FAIL→`completion_verifier_fail_v1` / PASS→`completion_verifier_pass_v1`) and (2) a new public query API `latest_pass_record()` — with no corresponding ADR update or CLAUDE.md/design-doc note. ADR 0043 is referenced for vocabulary only; neither the new PASS sidecar pattern nor the rationale for keeping two schemas instead of one versioned schema is captured anywhere for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## Schema-version strategy (explicit) / `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` scope entry / Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: A future maintainer adds a third verdict type and must infer the two-schema pattern from code rather than a documented decision
A consumer of the sidecar store wants to query both PASS and FAIL records and has no documented API contract for latest_pass_record()
The two-schema rationale (backwards compatibility) is not captured, so a future refactor may inadvertently merge schemas and break the existing test suite
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0044-completion-verifier-pass-sidecar.md` (or amend ADR 0043) covering: the PASS vs FAIL dual-schema decision and why a single versioned schema was rejected, the `criteria[]` contract (fields, `kind` vocabulary, nullability of `citation`), and the `latest_pass_record()` query API. Add this file to the Scope enumeration so it is not forgotten during implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define idempotency semantics for PASS sidecar emit: either guard with an existence check before write, use an upsert/overwrite policy, or document explicitly that duplicate records are tolerated and `latest_pass_record` is correct-by-ordering.
evidence: Plan says `build_payload` emits PASS records; `latest_pass_record` is defined but only reads the latest — implying an append/accumulate store
No mention of idempotency guard, upsert, or 'emit once per ticket' constraint
ABSENCE: no lifecycle ownership rule stating 'at most one PASS record per ticket' or cleanup of superseded records
findings.finding: The plan does not define idempotency for PASS sidecar records: if `transition_close` is invoked more than once for the same ticket (e.g., re-verification or retry), duplicate `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records will accumulate with no deduplication or upsert strategy.
scenarios: A ticket is re-closed after a reopen+re-verify cycle — two PASS records exist for the same ticket, making the audit trail ambiguous
Automated retry of a failed close command (e.g., transient I/O error before the manifest is signed) emits a second PASS record even though the first succeeded

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test oracle (to AC1 or a new AC) that injects a sidecar emit failure (e.g., emit raises RuntimeError) and asserts the ticket close completes successfully and returns the correct verdict — proving the best-effort/swallow contract is enforced.
evidence: Plan says: 'add a best-effort completion_sidecar.emit(result, ...) (swallow its own errors — persistence is observability, never load-bearing on the close)'
No AC includes a test where emit() raises and the close still succeeds
This is new behavioral contract: ticket close is unaffected by sidecar errors
findings.finding: No test oracle covers the error-swallowing behavior of the best-effort `completion_sidecar.emit()` call in `transition_close.py`: the plan states errors are swallowed ('swallow its own errors'), but no AC specifies a test that a sidecar emit failure does NOT abort or alter the ticket close outcome.
scenarios: Sidecar emit raises an unexpected exception (network error, schema validation failure, disk full); without a test the swallow behavior could accidentally be removed in a future refactor, breaking ticket closes
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2 or a new AC, add: 'A unit test patches completion_sidecar.emit to raise RuntimeError and confirms transition_close still returns a PASS verdict with no exception propagation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and document in the plan) that the workflow engine's structured-step output extraction automatically surfaces all fields of the `CompletionVerdict` model as named `steps.verify.outputs.*` keys, or add an explicit `outputs: [criteria]` declaration on the `verify` step in `completion-verification.yaml` if the engine requires it.
evidence: Plan: 'in the `completion_reconcile` step's `with:`, thread `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` (alongside the existing `raw_verdict`/`raw_findings`)'

Existing YAML (lines 58-64): wires `raw_verdict: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.verdict }}`, `raw_findings: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.findings }}`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id` — these are implicit field extractions from the structured output

ABSENCE: no plan text specifies whether `criteria` is automatically surfaced as `steps.verify.outputs.criteria` when the agent emits it in the `CompletionVerdict` structured output, or whether an explicit step `outputs:` key declaration is needed
findings.finding: The plan threads `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` from the `verify` prompt step into `completion_reconcile`, but the `verify` step uses `mode: structured` with `output_schema: completion_verdict`. The workflow engine must surface `criteria` as a named step output key for `${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` to resolve. The plan does not confirm that the engine's output-extraction mechanism (which currently extracts `verdict`, `findings`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id` as named keys from a structured step) will also surface `criteria` automatically, or whether an explicit `outputs:` declaration is needed on the `verify` step in the YAML.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml` — `verify` step + `reconcile` step `with:` block (lines 41-64 of existing YAML)
scenarios: Engine resolves `steps.verify.outputs.criteria` as undefined/null because `criteria` is not listed as an extracted output key — `raw_criteria` arrives as null in `completion_reconcile`, and `criteria[]` is silently dropped from the reconciled verdict even when the agent populated it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a diagnostic fallback in `build_payload` (e.g., a `logger.warning` when `verdict == 'PASS'` and `criteria` is empty) so operators can detect when the agent is not populating `criteria[]`, and consider adding a `criteria_populated` boolean flag to the sidecar payload.
evidence: Plan: 'PASS → `SCHEMA_PASS` + `criteria[]`; FAIL → unchanged `SCHEMA_FAIL` + `findings`'
Plan: '`criteria[]` is OPTIONAL — an older agent/response that omits it yields today's behavior'
ABSENCE: no fallback behavior (warning log, `criteria_populated: false` flag, or skip-emit) is specified for the PASS + empty-criteria case
findings.finding: The `build_payload` branch for PASS in `completion_sidecar.py` emits `criteria: result.get('criteria', [])`, but `criteria[]` is OPTIONAL (defaults to `[]`) in the `CompletionVerdict` model. The plan specifies no fallback or diagnostic when a PASS verdict arrives with an empty `criteria[]` — a case that is valid at the schema level (an older agent or one that failed to populate the field) but silently produces a `completion_verifier_pass_v1` record that is indistinguishable from a ticket with zero criteria. `latest_pass_record` consumers will see an empty `criteria[]` with no indication that the agent failed to emit it.
scenarios: A real LLM agent consistently fails to emit `criteria[]` (due to prompt ambiguity) — every close produces a `completion_verifier_pass_v1` record with `criteria: []`, and the per-criterion audit silently provides no value with no observable signal

findings.finding: The plan claims 'no YAML change' is needed because the workflow path 'pass[es] the verdict through `reconcile_verdict` + `findings.validate_structured` (which do NOT strip extra fields)' — but the actual bottleneck is earlier: the `completion-verification.yaml` step `reconcile` only wires six named outputs (`raw_verdict`, `raw_findings`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id`, `certifiable`) from the agent step into `completion_reconcile`, and `completion_reconcile` builds `result` from only those named `ctx.inputs` keys. A `criteria[]` field emitted by the agent in its structured output never reaches the `completion_reconcile` op's `result` dict — it is silently absent at the `with:` wiring stage, before `reconcile_verdict` or `validate_structured` ever see it. The YAML must be updated to wire `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` and `completion_reconcile` must read `ctx.inputs.get('raw_criteria')` and copy it onto `result['criteria']`.
findings.suggested_fix: In `completion-verification.yaml` reconcile step `with:` block add `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}`. In `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` add `"raw_criteria": { "type": "array", "description": "Agent-emitted per-criterion met records." }` to `properties` (and optionally relax `additionalProperties`). In `gate_ops.py:completion_reconcile`, after building `result`, add: `raw_criteria = ctx.inputs.get('raw_criteria'); if raw_criteria: result['criteria'] = list(raw_criteria)`.

findings.finding: The workflow path through `completion-verification.yaml` + `gate_ops.py::completion_reconcile` will silently drop `criteria[]`. The `reconcile` step's `with:` block only wires `raw_verdict`, `raw_findings`, `summary`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id`, and `certifiable`; and `completion_reconcile` in `gate_ops.py` hard-codes exactly those fields into `result` (line 125–133). `criteria[]` from the agent is never forwarded. The plan states 'no YAML change' is needed, but this is incorrect — both `completion-verification.yaml` (to wire `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}`) and `gate_ops.py::completion_reconcile` (to read `ctx.inputs.get('raw_criteria')` and copy it onto `result`) must be updated for `criteria[]` to survive the production workflow path.
findings.location: Scope section ('completion-verification workflow step ... no YAML change') / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml lines 58–68 / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_ops.py lines 125–133
findings.suggested_fix: In `completion-verification.yaml` reconcile `with:` block add `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}`. In `gate_ops.py::completion_reconcile`, after building `result`, add: `raw_criteria = ctx.inputs.get('raw_criteria') or []; if raw_criteria: result['criteria'] = raw_criteria`. Also update `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` to allow `raw_criteria` (or change `additionalProperties` to `true`).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'once contracts.py carries criteria[] the workflow path preserves it with no YAML change,' but this is mechanically incorrect: the production completion-verification path routes through the `completion_reconcile` step in `gate_ops.py`, which builds its `result` dict only from explicitly named `ctx.inputs` keys (raw_verdict, raw_findings, summary, runner, model, trace_id, certifiable). Because `raw_criteria` is never read from `ctx.inputs`, never wired in `completion-verification.yaml`, and `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` has `additionalProperties: false`, the agent-emitted `criteria[]` is silently discarded at the reconcile boundary. The PASS sidecar would always receive an empty criteria array regardless of what the agent emitted.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add a fourth enumerated edit target: `src/rebar/schemas/completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` — add `raw_criteria: { type: array }` to properties and remove the blocking `additionalProperties: false` or relax it; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml` — add `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` to the reconcile step's `with:` block; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_ops.py` — in `completion_reconcile`, after building `result`, add `criteria = ctx.inputs.get('raw_criteria') or []; if criteria: result['criteria'] = criteria`.

findings.finding: The plan incorrectly claims the workflow path preserves `criteria[]` with "no YAML change", but the actual `completion_reconcile` step manually assembles its `result` dict from named inputs and never reads a `raw_criteria` key — and `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` has `"additionalProperties": false`, which would reject any attempt to pass `raw_criteria` through the YAML without a schema change. The agent's `criteria[]` is therefore silently dropped on the workflow (gate) path.
findings.location: Scope section — paragraph on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (reconcile_verdict) and the closing paragraph claiming no YAML change needed; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_ops.py` (`completion_reconcile`); `src/rebar/schemas/completion_reconcile_input.schema.json`
findings.suggested_fix: In `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json`, add `"raw_criteria": {"type": "array", "description": "The agent's raw criteria list (optional)."}` (no schema-required change; `additionalProperties: false` must be relaxed or the key added). In `gate_ops.py`, add `result["criteria"] = list(ctx.inputs.get("raw_criteria") or [])` after line 133. In the YAML, add `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` to the reconcile `with:` block.

findings.finding: The best-effort/error-swallowing behavior added to the PASS path in `transition_close.py` (sidecar emit errors are swallowed so they never break the close) has no corresponding test specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` paragraph; also Acceptance Criteria (none address this path)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2 or a dedicated unit test for `transition_close`, inject a sidecar that raises on `emit()` and assert that the close returns successfully and the error is logged but not re-raised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the YAML scope to explicitly describe how the verify step exposes `criteria` as a named output (e.g., adding an `outputs:` block to the verify step or extracting it from the step's result JSON) so that `${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` is guaranteed non-empty when the agent returns a populated array.
evidence: Plan says: 'thread `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}`' but the verify-step output wiring is never described
Scope section enumerates the YAML change only as adding to the reconcile step's `with:` — no mention of adding `criteria` to the verify step's `outputs:` block
The gate_ops.py scope reads `raw_criteria` from `ctx.inputs`, consistent with the reconcile step receiving it — but that depends on the verify step actually emitting it as a named output
findings.finding: The YAML scope describes adding `raw_criteria: ${{ steps.verify.outputs.criteria }}` to the reconcile step's `with:` block, but nowhere in the plan is it described how the verify step exposes `criteria` as a named output (i.e., how `steps.verify.outputs.criteria` is populated). If the verify step emits a monolithic JSON blob and `criteria` is not extracted and re-emitted as a separate step output, the expression resolves to empty/undefined, silently dropping all per-criterion records.
scenarios: Implementer adds `raw_criteria` to the reconcile `with:` block but forgets to wire `criteria` as an output of the verify step; the expression is undefined; `raw_criteria` is always empty; AC#2's integration test may pass if it stubs the verify step output directly, masking the production bug
findings.suggested_fix: In the `completion-verification.yaml` scope paragraph, add: 'Also add a `criteria` entry to the verify step's `outputs:` mapping (extracting it from the structured result JSON) so that `steps.verify.outputs.criteria` is a valid, populated expression.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent structured log/metric) in the swallowed-error path of the PASS sidecar emit so failures are observable without being load-bearing.
evidence: Plan says: 'add a best-effort `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=…, repo_root=repo_root)` (swallow its own errors — persistence is observability, never load-bearing on the close)'
No mention of a warning log, metric increment, or trace event when the emit fails
ABSENCE: no observability instrumentation specified for the new failure point
findings.finding: The plan instructs `transition_close.py` to swallow sidecar emit errors with no accompanying log/metric/trace, leaving operators blind to silent PASS-record write failures.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py (Scope section) and Compatibility & Rollback section
scenarios: Sidecar storage becomes unavailable (disk full, permission error, path misconfiguration) — every PASS silently drops its per-criterion record with no signal to operators
Debugging a missing audit trail becomes impossible because there is no logged evidence that writes were attempted and failed
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the emit call in a try/except and log `logger.warning('completion_sidecar PASS emit failed for ticket %s: %s', resolved_id, exc)` — one line, keeps it non-load-bearing but observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `build_payload` scope bullet, explicitly enumerate adding `'criteria': v.get('criteria', [])` to the PASS branch's returned dict (the current explicit-key return does not forward it automatically).
evidence: completion_sidecar.py lines 156-166: build_payload returns an explicit dict with 'schema','verdict','ticket_id','findings','remediation','certifiable','runner','model','material_fingerprint' — no 'criteria' key
Plan: 'build_payload branches on the verdict (PASS → SCHEMA_PASS + criteria[]; FAIL → unchanged SCHEMA_FAIL + findings)' — but the explicit return dict structure is not updated in the plan text
findings.finding: `build_payload` in `completion_sidecar.py` makes a shallow copy of `verdict` then calls `reconcile_verdict(v)` on that copy. `reconcile_verdict` does not know about `criteria[]` and does not preserve it — it only sets/reads `verdict`, `findings`, and `remediation`. Even if the caller passes a verdict with `criteria[]`, `build_payload` then cherry-picks explicit keys (lines 157-166), none of which is `criteria`. The plan says `build_payload` should branch on verdict and carry `criteria[]` on PASS, but the current explicit-key pattern means `criteria` must be explicitly added to the returned dict — the plan does not enumerate this cherry-pick update.
scenarios: PASS sidecar is emitted without `criteria[]` even after all other changes, defeating the audit purpose of the feature
findings.suggested_fix: In the PASS branch of build_payload, add `'criteria': v.get('criteria', []) or []` to the returned payload dict alongside the existing keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' or 'Schema-version strategy' section explaining WHY `criteria[]` is a parallel sibling to `findings[]` (e.g., because `findings` is failures-only by contract and adding `met=true` entries would violate that invariant, requiring callers to filter by `met` to get the FAIL list — a breaking semantic change).
evidence: Plan says 'a positive per-criterion `criteria[]` array — one `{criterion, met, citation, kind}` entry per acceptance/success criterion evaluated' with no stated rationale for this shape vs. alternatives.
Schema-version rationale IS present: 'Do NOT bump the existing FAIL schema. Keep `SCHEMA_FAIL` unchanged' — but that explains the versioning approach, not the parallel-array shape.
ABSENCE: no sentence explains why criteria[] is a sibling to findings[] rather than, e.g., annotating findings with met=true entries or returning a single unified array with a 'met' discriminant.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the core architectural choice of a parallel `criteria[]` array alongside the existing `findings[]`, rather than alternative structures. The schema-version rationale is given but the overall 'why this shape' is absent, leaving the implementer without justification for a potentially surprising design (two separate arrays on the same verdict object).
scenarios: An implementer asked 'why not unify findings and criteria into one array with a met field?' has no plan text to cite, increasing the chance of a divergent implementation decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'The parallel-array shape is required because `findings` is contractually failures-only (reconcile_verdict enforces this; callers rely on it); merging met-criteria into `findings` with a `met` discriminant would break every caller that iterates findings as a failure list. A sibling array preserves the existing contract exactly.'

findings.finding: The completion-verification.yaml workflow and gate_ops.py (completion_reconcile step) are unenumerated consumers that must be updated to thread criteria[] from the agent's output through to the reconciled verdict. The YAML wires only `raw_verdict`, `raw_findings`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id` from the verify step; `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` has `additionalProperties: false` with no `raw_criteria` field.

`completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` has `"additionalProperties": false` (line 18), which means any `raw_criteria` field added to the YAML `with:` block for the `reconcile` step would be rejected at schema-validation time in the workflow engine. This schema is not named as an edit target in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` properties: `"raw_criteria": { "type": "array", "description": "The agent's per-criterion met-records (one per evaluated criterion)." }` and keep `additionalProperties: false`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
